sentences.text: Hello and welcome to another episode of the Fluency Fur podcast.
I'm here with a very special guest.
Hi, Carol, who is a family friend.
Hi, Carol.
Hi, hi.
So at the start of every episode, we start small talk.
So, Carol, firstly, thank you so much for doing this.
It's a pleasure.
I know you are a very busy person, but I'm so grateful that we can schedule this.
Me too.
I'm excited.
I'm looking forward to it.
So how are you?
I'm good.
Thank you.
I've been away for a few days in Worcestershire in the Malvern and I
spent some time with a friend and I went to Stratford-on-Avon Shakespeare's
birthplace to see not a Shakespeare play, but a modern play, which is funny.
Very good.
Sounds interesting.
And recently you went on holiday.
I did.
Where did you go?
I went to Slovenia first time, which was really interesting.
It's a tiny country in terms of comparing it with Britain, about the
size of Wales.
It's only about 200 miles at the greatest length.
I didn't know it was that small.
New Jersey, comparing it with New Jersey is another date, I guess.
Right, right.
And it's very wooded, mountainous, green, green in two ways.
So it's green because there are loads of pine tree, countryside, but
also environmentally conscious.
So the capital, Lublyana, there's no lid.
Sounds amazing.
It is really beautiful.
And what was the food like?
It was very cosmopolitan, actually.
I think, I think traditionally I probably wouldn't have loved it.
Quite heavy, manic food, and I don't eat meat.
So, but because there's a lot of international influence, there's a lot
of good continental style cooks there in the capital.
So now it's time for the next topic, which is family action, because
Carol, you are a family friend, right?
I know you through my parents.
So do you remember the early days of getting to know our family?
I do, yes.
I remember coming to one of Rachel's, it could have been her
first birthday party, or certainly under three in the back garden
of Conallan Avenue with your wider family there.
So I remember Wendy's sister there and all the kids playing in the garden
and Wendy's effortless hospitality.
My mum always used to say, Wendy's amazing, isn't she?
So relaxed, so relaxed.
So my mum got to know your family through coming up here
at Christmas and spending the evening.
So, yes, how did I get to know you?
So I got to know Ian Moore because we ran quite a lot of training.
And then he offered me some work.
When I wanted to leave my day, he offered me six weeks work.
And that was my jumping off point.
I decided, right, I'm going to go.
Wow.
And so then I did some work for him and we worked together on there.
And with Wendy, I got to know she came to the women's group.
I can't remember how often we met, but let's say once a month
we talked about deep personal stuff and we shared all our ups and downs.
We shared our inner lives with each other.
And Trish, who you also know, and I were running that group.
And we used a book that was written by some experts,
women's developers and so we asked searching questions of each other.
Yeah, I mean, just so everyone knows, we went on a lot of family,
joint family holidays and I have a lot of fond memories in France with you.
You also liked French and obviously at Christmas time,
we'd have parties here and at our house.
So, yeah, I also have a great time.
Yeah, I mean, the holidays were incredible
because we've spent quite a lot of holidays either in Brittany in France
or on the Ile de Ré, that very gorgeous island cycling room.
And Harry, my son, used to love talking to David,
seeing him play the guitar.
I think it was a bit of a looked up to you in the early days,
I think, you know, when he was younger and you were older.
And those holidays were such fun and they were also a lifeline for me
because they were a way of me having a holiday as a woman
who later became divorced.
So when my marriage changed, I came on holiday with your family and my child.
And we were really, really enveloped in that family environment.
And I also got to know your grandma, you know,
and I've loved getting to know them, and my mum loved getting to know them.
So we actually were on holiday in France.
My mum, your grandparents and all of you,
I've got pictures of her bowling on the beach with Peter Grant.
I do remember the holiday house in France.
And the other thing I've been interested in is that also as you've grown up,
you know, I've got to know your nearest and dearest
and also helped certainly Emily and Rachel think about their work,
practice interviews and things.
I've been very honoured as a family friend to do those things.
You've helped all of us and you were also the celebrant at my wedding.
How do you describe yourself?
Well, it's the person who takes care of the whole sort of ceremony.
Although I'm not a vicar or a priest and I wasn't officially marrying you,
I was seeing to it that it went in the way that you and Jean wanted
and that it included everybody.
And yeah, it was the most incredible thing to be asked.
I was so honoured to be asked and I loved doing it.
I nearly forgot to ask you to say something.
What might I do?
Oh, yeah.
The main bit.
Yeah.
It was a magical day, wasn't it?
It was.
Very, very special.
And I was there.
So let's move on to travel.
So you've travelled quite extensively, I would say.
Is there a place that changed you instantly?
That's a good question.
It's interesting because I'm going to talk about somewhere
that isn't that far away and it's Cornwall.
Cornwall.
Sounding house.
Well, I think over time.
I went to Cornwall first on my honeymoon husband.
I went there because his family had lived there
when my mother-in-law's father was ill.
Maybe he could recover better in that climate in the old days.
And so there were lots of stories and pictures about Cornwall,
that part of Cornwall.
I spent a week there and I fell in love with that part of the country
and I subsequently went back on holidays with Harry and Martin.
And then also some really tough things happened.
So my marriage ended and I was in Cornwall when it ended.
So that really was tough.
So going back to Cornwall after that
was really full of many memories that were difficult.
And I felt I had to change my relationship to those memories
to be able to keep good memories despite the break in the marriage
and to reclaim Cornwall for myself.
And so I went back quite a lot with Harry and with other friends.
And I feel it's a place that I relate to in the next part
rather than always looking back to how it was.
This picture, I love this picture
because the perspectives are all kind of odd, isn't it?
But it's a picture of St Ives I talked about elsewhere.
And it's just a lovely kind of breezy spring day, I think.
Looking out at the sea through a window.
That's always nice, isn't it?
A shot through a window.
I like that.
I like that.
Little boats on the sea.
And it's sort of very naive in way looking.
Very sort of odd perspective.
Colourful, friendly, cheerful, yeah.
And what's one country or moment from your travel that you will never...
You see Europe quite a lot.
But memorably, Japan, India and Peru.
No longer all trips.
What will I never forget?
No, a moment doesn't have to be the moment, does it?
I think being in Japan,
Japan's kind of like top of my list for different places.
It's such a mix of ancient and modern.
And, you know, you've got...
So I remember, actually, I can remember this.
Tell a little story, is that OK?
So, Gene, my friend, dear friend and I that you know,
we were travelling in Japan.
And we were in the mountains.
And we were booked into this hotel that we were told
the Emperor had stayed in this hotel.
And it was a bit shabby these days.
It was a bit run down.
But the tour company said,
we think you'll really love it.
We love it.
We think we should go there.
So we got there.
We were tired.
We were emotional.
Gene wasn't well.
And we walked into this place.
And I thought, one, there was...
We were greeted with a big fabulous feast of food,
but we were too tired.
And she was not well enough to enjoy.
And I remember...
And this is a big mystery.
You don't like that.
We'll have to move on more quickly.
Anyway, the next morning, when she was feeling better
and I was feeling refreshed,
we took a look around this place
and it had one of those onsen baths
where you can relax.
And it was the women's turn to have one morning
to the men's bath.
And it was set in the most beautiful Japanese garden.
So you could sit in these baths,
looking out at these incredibly beautiful Japanese trees
in this sort of beautifully shaped garden,
not far from Mount Fuji.
And sit there.
And I just thought, what a difference 24 hours makes.
Yesterday, I was tired and crab and bad tempered.
And today, I said to myself,
go from one extreme to the other.
This is a painting by Val Hudson,
who's a dear friend of mine.
And kind of, I like the different textures in it.
She rubs...
She puts paint on.
She rubs wax on.
And then she kind of takes some of the wax off
and get these different textures.
I just love the kind of...
I think it's called Quiet Conversation.
You get that sort of meditative feel.
And you get drawn into it by the different colors.
Just the depth of color that this involves me in.
And in the morning, when I look at it,
I just feel a sense of relaxation.
Tell me what it was like growing up in London.
You grew up in London, right?
It was amazing growing up in London.
Was it?
Yeah.
So what did you like about it?
I loved...
I lived in Southeast London,
which is a very big green area called Blacklea.
Very ancient heathland.
It borders onto Greenwich Park,
which is also a very big space of green.
Royal Greenwich Park.
And it was a place where Henry A.
Wallace,
in times gone by,
there's lots of history on both Blackheath and in Greenwich
and lots of greenery on the edge of London.
So it's not cramped.
It's not...
It's very spacious.
And Blackheath Village, as it's called,
is an old village.
And so it's not like...
It doesn't look like a classic old English village
in the country, but it was village-like.
In the 1950s when I grew up there,
I lived on what we now call a social housing estate,
which was newly built along with a newly built school
that was very close to the heath to all the greenery.
So what kind of commune was that social housing estate?
I would call that working club.
And some from a work ground.
And before my parents moved into that two-bedroom
apartment, we called it a flam.
The housing estate was quite small.
It wasn't a load of tower block.
It was full of people who were...
There were some people who'd fled,
I think, in the war from places
where they were ostracized, Jewish people.
There were lots of working-class people
who were getting their first home
with a good rent and a nice place to live.
And they were purpose-built, so they had...
Often, you would be in, perhaps,
other things and sharing toilets
and whatever with other people.
I didn't do that, but we lived with my grandma
when we moved there.
But here, it was a purpose-built flat
with its own bathroom, with a balcony.
It was a nice place to live.
And Blackheath is quite a posh place.
There's quite a lot of money in Blackheath.
Now or...
Always, yeah.
So there's some incredibly beautiful buildings,
houses that were built.
So at the top of the roadway island,
there's a whole row of big Georgian-y out over.
So there's a lot of money there,
and there always has been.
So it was a place that you had a lot of people
who could afford nice things,
and Blackheath's shops reflected that.
But equally, there were several council estates,
social housing estates, around the railway line.
There was a railway line 20 minutes into central London.
And the newly built school was a state school.
And in Blackheath, well, near to Blackheath,
there were also schools that were public schools
where their children could go.
So in Blackheath, you have a mixture,
as you do in most places in England,
a working-class, middle-class,
only class, I would call them.
And a lot of people think class isn't a thing anymore.
I think it's still there.
I think it's changed.
It's still...
So you grew up working class,
but you went to grammar, right?
I did.
That's where you pass a test, the 11 plus,
and you get accepted to the school.
And I heard, because your accent,
you know, it's quite unique.
You had elocution, right?
Yeah.
What was that like?
It's funny because they didn't call them
elocution classes.
So the girls whose parents had aspirations
for them to talk properly,
you know, if you're...
Properly.
Properly.
So I'm actually a Cockney by birth,
because I was born within the Sound of Bobel.
But most, nobody would think that I'm a Cockney,
that I speak.
Well, I can talk like that if I wanna, right?
But my sister had a very different way of speaking to me
because of our school.
We did have speech and drama lessons every Tuesday
throughout one of my years at school.
And that meant a whole class of girls
had to stand on the stage one by one
and talk to the audience of the rest of the class,
project their voices to the back of the school hall
and say what you'd been...
Pick a subject and talk about it.
Could be what you'd been doing that weekend.
You had to talk for a certain amount of time.
You had to stand well
and you had to give this kind of speech.
We also had some book which trained our accent.
So we set, we had books, I'm not kidding you,
which said things like,
the rain in Spain falls mainly on the plain.
The rain in Spain
rains mainly in the rain.
Classic line.
The classic line, which is in Pygmalion,
talking about training a girl to talk properly.
So there was this underlying sense
that they were doing, it was a good thing to be done.
I didn't ever question it.
On the other hand, what it leaves you with
is a little bit of a crazy mixed up kid around your identity
because I don't sound like my family sounds.
And often when you sound like this,
people think you're stuck up or a snob
that you're looking down on,
that you're somehow not real in some way
because I've learned to talk like this.
I think it's interesting putting it on, yeah.
So of course, and of course we were taught to do that.
So it wasn't our natural way of speaking
and all the girls had it,
but in some ways it stuck with me.
I don't know why it stuck with me.
And I think because I was interested in my education
and as I went to university,
I think you get under the word,
you kind of get messages really,
that in order to fit in and to get on,
there is something about the way that you speak.
And certainly in the fifties,
when I was growing up in the sixties,
there weren't lots of different accents on telly,
on radio, very, very posh RP, received pronunciation.
When I came to Sheffield,
I did stick out like a sore thumb.
So you said when you came to Sheffield,
stuck out like a sore thumb,
great phrase to learn everyone.
How did that feel?
Can you just tell me a little bit about that?
It felt, I mean, I was sort of used to that in a way.
I wasn't actually, no, it was very noticeable
because Sheffield's a very working class city
and you're in Yorkshire and in Yorkshire.
It's not just that you have the short A's
like bath and grab and pass,
but you also have quite a lot of ways
of saying things that are particular to Yorkshire.
A lot of dialect words.
A lot of dialect words.
And I was working with,
I was going around and talking to 16 to 24 year olds
who were unemployed and often young men.
And they, so there were a number of things there.
They were often shy and embarrassed
and sort of looking at their boots
and not looking me in the eye.
And also when they did talk,
they spoke quite quickly with this Yorkshire accent,
but also with the dialect.
So you couldn't understand that.
I could understand them
because I'd been an English teacher
and I was very interested in dialect.
So I've got a good ear for that kind of thing.
And I picked it up, but initially it was harder work.
It's like learning English in a class
and then going to England
and to really listen to how it's spoken.
And it was like that with them.
So it took me a while.
And then of course I was definitely not like them.
So when you've got difference like that
and there are all those things
that people associate with how you talk.
Like as I said before,
people think you're posh that you're stuck up,
better than they are.
All those things that go with that
make the actual rapport building
and the relationship building
with someone you don't know quite hard.
You've got to, you know,
I felt I've had to work quite hard building relationships
to see beneath, beyond that.
Beyond the accent.
The judgements, yeah, about the accent.
You only have kind of like the identity,
you don't use the word crisis,
but that's the phrase.
Identity crisis about your accent when you were young
or you had that throughout?
I didn't feel it was a crisis
because for me by then it was normal,
but it was a sense of feeling uncomfortable
in a new place where I didn't belong.
And it's a bit like when you read a novel,
you know, and the stranger comes to town.
It was a bit like that.
And there were a lot of Southerners who came up
at the same time as I did.
A bit of an exodus from London
because the government wanted to bring people
to Sheffield to do jobs,
to create jobs outside of London.
And so there was some resentment
about the pushed house prices.
It wasn't just how I talked,
it was kind of like,
oh, you're one of them.
People from London who've stolen our house.
So it was a whole thing.
But I think, I mean, accent,
I'm grateful for what I've had.
It has.
In the time that I grew up,
there were advantages to not being judged
by working-class.
When I went to university,
I think it helped me fit in there
in a way that, you know,
I didn't even realize I would need,
I had no idea that I would need to fit.
Interesting because I'm from Sheffield,
born and bred in Sheffield,
and I don't really speak like other people from Sheffield.
I've always had the problem with my identity.
So occasionally, if I go out,
or speak with a broader accent,
I will change my accent.
It's difficult to know what my accent really is
because it's always depending on who I'm with.
Yeah, I think that's natural.
When I go home,
I still say home for London,
if I'm in a taxi with a taxi driver,
I will end up sounding not broad-cockneyed,
but I will enjoy getting away.
It's a part of report building, isn't it?
You kind of follow the pace of somebody.
So there's quite a clipped pace in London,
quite quick speaking, and staccato,
and you do the glottal stop, you know.
So there are things that I do.
Don't you just explain glottal stop, like bottle?
Yeah, bottle.
So you don't say bottle,
which you would say if you were trying to talk properly
in inverted commas,
you say bottle, or uh-uh.
Gotta do that, bottle of water.
So you say your T's at the back of your throat,
not with your tongue behind your T, bottle.
Your accent is quite unique.
Do people ever comment, even now, I wonder,
do people comment on it now?
Yes, a taxi driver commented on it the other day.
He said to me, you're not from around here, are you?
Where did you grow up?
And so, and actually, it's interesting.
How did that make you feel?
So I thought, oh, actually,
nobody's asked me that recently,
so I was happy to talk about it.
He was a nice guy, I didn't take any offense,
and I think it was just genuine curiosity.
It's interesting, because you say my accent's
quite unique, in a way,
what the training that I got at school
to talk in a particular way was to make you
like everybody else who spoke that way.
Right.
Alfie.
Yeah, I received pronunciation.
So it's interesting that you hear it as unique,
so what do you hear?
So what I mean is, it's unique for the area.
When I was a kid, you had this vibe feeling
of being a teacher, because of the way you spoke.
I kind of always admired the way you spoke.
Yeah, I guess it's a mixture of things, isn't it?
It's like how you pronounce the words,
but also there are other differences
in the way that we speak.
But it's not just your accent,
you're also quite, I think this podcast
will become an advanced English podcast.
It might be quite difficult for some people.
Sorry, everybody.
No, no, no, but it's good.
The words you're using are amazing.
Some of the phrases you're using.
As a lifelong English teacher,
I'm always listening not only to people's accents,
but also to their word choices,
their phrase, their idiosyncrasies.
Some people have specific words
that only they say or they use.
I just think it's absolutely fascinating.
Yeah, I mean, it's lovely to hear you.
I'm interested in what you hear and what they say.
I'm interested in that too, as an ex-English teacher,
not teaching how to learn the language,
teaching language and literature to young.
And because my degree was in that,
because I taught in that for five years,
because I've always read and studied literature,
I love words.
And you kind of pick them up,
you don't know you've got them.
And when people do tell me,
I'm articulate is what they say.
And I don't know where that comes from.
I just have that.
I've just learned.
Well, you have a natural instinct
to choose more specific words if you describe.
And that's what I love, you know.
I've got loads of them in my pocket.
So I like to use as many of them.
I don't do it consciously.
I don't do it consciously.
I just love words.
Yeah, absolutely.
And you're a big fan of Shakespeare.
I am.
And of course you write, you're a avid reader.
Do you think Acts still says something about plan?
Yeah, I do.
So Harry, my son who's born in Sheffield,
a Yorkshireman, he's got short A's,
but he talks well, like you do, in inverting.
We say well, so there's a judgment in there, isn't there?
It tells you that language is related to plan.
However, his friends who've gone to boarding school
and who have got very RP accents, let's say,
some of those friends, I can't say for certain,
but one story that he's told me
is that people say to him,
your accent's really strong, Harry.
And it's because of the vowel,
because of the vowel sounds.
And in that is a judgment.
So I think we can kid ourselves.
So I think there's lots of things about class
that have changed.
So education's become more available
irrespective of your class.
Jobs have become more available.
We see more representation from different classes
on TV and on the radio.
Nonetheless, people are still often interested
in your background.
And voice is one of the quickest ways
that you start to get a sense of it.
Yeah, it's interesting because I don't think
I can ever say far-off.
It has to be bad.
I can't change it and I never will.
You shouldn't have to.
But my you, so like,
I started saying cup rather than cook.
I haven't started at that.
I mean, that started a long time.
That did change in my accent, I noticed.
But yeah, accent definitely does say something.
Do you like that?
I think if it was free of judgment
and kind of assumptions about,
it's where people go to next,
you talk like this, therefore, dot, dot, dot,
it means this.
So if it was more about, wow, that's different,
that's varied, that's rich,
you know, how did you get to speak like that?
But it's not, it's kind of like there is a sense
that, you know, there's something missing
or there's a lack or there's a shortfall.
So I think it's because of the next step,
the meaning that we attach to that
or that some people attach to that.
I think there's less meaning attached to that.
Sometimes the way that you speak,
people assume that you're not as bright
if you speak a certain way.
People see that voice is another way to sort of sort.
Do you sound like me or not?
You don't sound like me.
It can be a conclusion that people go,
so can I trust you?
Do I like you?
All those things, it's not the voice itself,
it's what people infer from how you talk.
And often those things are not good
or they are good if you talk in a certain way.
She's one of us.
People like our praise that we sort of talk like.
Oh yeah, yeah.
Is she a person?
Is she PLU?
Ha, ha, ha, ha, ha, ha.
Really?
Oh, goodness me.
Do you ever feel pressure sound
or a certain way in school or work?
I don't remember feeling any pressure at school.
I think I just didn't, I didn't question it.
I just did the classes.
But I don't think I did.
I was schooled by then, that's how I spoke.
I mean, there's a way of talking light and professional,
isn't there, as well as the accent that you've got.
So you have to kind of tune into those things
and know, you know, I worked in a theater box office
and you know, you have to give service
and work in a particular way.
My sort of Saturday job to earn some pocket money.
So pick those things up about this is what it means
to be in hospitality or in service to others.
So you've got to behave in a certain way
in how you talk.
You don't say, yeah, you want.
You know, say, good evening, sir.
How can I help you?
So interesting.
My mom's first job was working in the market,
the vegetable market.
She also had to speak in a certain way,
have good banter, and very kind of the same,
but opposite.
So there were expectations of how you should speak.
Like if she's trying to speak very properly.
I'd have given her a wide berth.
I mean, it's emotionally intelligent.
You adjust your language in the same way
that you speak a different language
in a different country.
I like that in environments, isn't it?
There's a certain way of vocabulary,
a dialect for different jobs, even.
So it is time for the last set,
which is the reflective and what I call challenge.
So take your time.
Don't feel pressured to answer right away.
So the first question is,
what's one thing you learned
that you wish more people understood?
Good question.
I don't know.
I think I'm not a very patient person.
I think the importance of patience is really valuable.
So giving time to others.
Sticking on the roads, for example,
that kind of courtesy.
But I think patience and courtesy, yeah.
Patience, and there's something about in the NHS,
there's this word civility,
and there's been a lot of research
that civility saves lives,
that when you talk to each other well,
and kindly and politely,
and patience, it saves lives.
I'm a big fan of kindness, politeness, and passion.
Yeah, they all fit together, don't they?
Next, has your idea of happiness gained as an older?
Yeah, I think it has.
I think I've been lucky
to have had a lot of good things in my life,
and experiences particularly.
And I've earned reasonably well,
and I've been able to support myself
and have the things that I wanted.
So I suppose in many ways,
I've been able to satisfy a lot of the things
that I might have yearned for,
and that in itself is a kind of happiness, isn't it?
But I also realise the value of simplicity
and how important that is to me,
and how, yeah, I think having time to really savour things
and take pleasure in small things really matters.
Good food with friends,
done a lot of that with your family,
really important, small pleasure.
Even average is great too, right?
Exactly, ordinary food.
Bread and cheese and wine.
Exactly.
Who was it that said that?
Properties, all I need is just cheese and wine,
bread, cheese, wine, and some friends.
Great.
I don't know what the quotas are.
I'll try and find out.
The next is, if you could talk to your younger self,
what would-
Trust your instincts more.
Trust your-
Would you like to elaborate?
Yeah, I think there are times when you have a sense
that something is right for you,
and I think there have been times
when I've really acted on that,
even when I was younger, saying no to something.
I didn't know why I was saying no to it,
but I think it was a good no.
Equally, I think if you have a concern about something
that you brush aside, that keeps reappearing,
then your instinct is telling you something,
and I've done that.
I mean, I wish I hadn't.
